inline_review:
commit 4aea02eb0198d85649c0ca70488df26ca690e5ea
Author: Donet Tom <donettom@linux.ibm.com>

memory tiering: Do not allow promotion if NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING is disabled

This commit aims to prevent promotion of pages from lower tier to higher
tier nodes when NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING is disabled, while still
allowing normal NUMA balancing migrations within the same tier.

Fixes: 33024536bafd ("memory tiering: hot page selection with hint page fault latency")

> diff --git a/kernel/sched/fair.c b/kernel/sched/fair.c
> index 0a35a82e4792..03306b6384fd 100644
> --- a/kernel/sched/fair.c
> +++ b/kernel/sched/fair.c
> @@ -2094,8 +2094,12 @@ bool should_numa_migrate_memory(struct task_struct *p, struct folio *folio,
>  	this_cpupid = cpu_pid_to_cpupid(dst_cpu, current->pid);
>  	last_cpupid = folio_xchg_last_cpupid(folio, this_cpupid);
>  
> +	/*
> +	 * Do not allow promotion if NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING is disabled
> +	 * and the pages are on the lower tier.
> +	 */
>  	if (!(sysctl_numa_balancing_mode & NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING) &&
> -	    !node_is_toptier(src_nid) && !cpupid_valid(last_cpupid))
> +	    !node_is_toptier(src_nid))
>  		return false;

Does this logic unintentionally block all migrations originating from a
lower-tier node, even if the destination is in the same tier?

The commit message states that if NUMA_BALANCING_NORMAL is enabled, migration
will occur only between nodes within the same memory tier. However, the check
only evaluates the source node. If the system has two lower-tier nodes,
wouldn't a migration between them also be blocked because
!node_is_toptier(src_nid) is true?

Could this also allow cross-tier migrations from top-tier nodes to lower-tier
nodes, since src_nid would be top-tier and bypass this check? If tasks move
to a lower-tier node (e.g., a compute node with slow memory), active pages
might migrate to the lower tier but then become permanently trapped there
because any future migration out of the lower-tier node is blocked by this
return statement.

Should this evaluate both the source and destination nodes, for example by
checking if node_is_toptier(src_nid) != node_is_toptier(dst_nid)?

Additionally, by removing the !cpupid_valid(last_cpupid) check, does this
completely disable normal locality-based NUMA balancing for lower-tier memory
when tiering is disabled?

Previously, disabling tiering would fall back to standard flat NUMA balancing.
Without the cpupid check, normal NUMA balancing might be permanently crippled
for lower-tier nodes instead of acting like flat NUMA.

commit 4aea02eb0198d85649c0ca70488df26ca690e5ea
Author: Donet Tom <donettom@linux.ibm.com>
Date:   Mon Mar 23 09:48:49 2026 +0000

    memory tiering: Do not allow promotion if NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING is disabled
    
    In the current implementation, if NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING is
    disabled and the pages are on the lower tier, the pages may still be
    promoted.
    
    This happens because task_numa_work() updates the last_cpupid field to
    record the last access time only when NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING is
    enabled and the folio is on the lower tier. If
    NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING is disabled, the last_cpupid field
    can retains a valid last CPU id.
    
    In should_numa_migrate_memory(), the decision checks whether
    NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING is disabled, the folio is on the lower
    tier, and last_cpupid is invalid. However, the last_cpupid can be
    valid when NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING is disabled, the condition
    evaluates to false and migration is allowed.
    
    This patch prevents promotion when NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING is
    disabled and the folio is on the lower tier.
    
    Behavior before this change:
    ============================
      - If NUMA_BALANCING_NORMAL is enabled, migration occurs between
        nodes within the same memory tier, and promotion from lower
        tier to higher tier may also happen.
    
      - If NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING is enabled, promotion from
        lower tier to higher tier nodes is allowed.
    
    Behavior after this change:
    ===========================
      - If NUMA_BALANCING_NORMAL is enabled, migration will occur only
        between nodes within the same memory tier.
    
      - If NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING is enabled, promotion from lower
        tier to higher tier nodes will be allowed.
    
      - If both NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING and NUMA_BALANCING_NORMAL are
        enabled, both migration (same tier) and promotion (cross tier) are
        allowed.
    
    Fixes: 33024536bafd ("memory tiering: hot page selection with hint page fault latency")
    Signed-off-by: Donet Tom <donettom@linux.ibm.com>

diff --git a/kernel/sched/fair.c b/kernel/sched/fair.c
index 0a35a82e4792..03306b6384fd 100644
--- a/kernel/sched/fair.c
+++ b/kernel/sched/fair.c
@@ -2094,8 +2094,12 @@ bool should_numa_migrate_memory(struct task_struct *p, struct folio *folio,
 	this_cpupid = cpu_pid_to_cpupid(dst_cpu, current->pid);
 	last_cpupid = folio_xchg_last_cpupid(folio, this_cpupid);
 
+	/*
+	 * Do not allow promotion if NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING is disabled
+	 * and the pages are on the lower tier.
+	 */
 	if (!(sysctl_numa_balancing_mode & NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING) &&
-	    !node_is_toptier(src_nid) && !cpupid_valid(last_cpupid))
+	    !node_is_toptier(src_nid))
 		return false;
 
 	/*

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Impact of a NUMA Balancing Change**

Okay, so I'm looking at this proposed commit, and immediately, I see it's dealing with a nuance in memory tiering and NUMA balancing interactions. The core problem is that with memory tiering disabled but normal NUMA balancing enabled, we *shouldn't* be promoting pages from lower tiers to higher tiers based on hotness. The old logic, however, seems to have a loophole. Let me break this down.

My initial thought is to zero in on how `last_cpupid` is involved. The key function here is `should_numa_migrate_memory()`, the gatekeeper for memory migrations. When tiering is off, we *should* simply be treating the system as regular NUMA. The commit message points out that the old conditional `!(sysctl_numa_balancing_mode & NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING) && !node_is_toptier(src_nid) && !cpupid_valid(last_cpupid)` could allow migration even when it shouldn't, due to the validity of `last_cpupid`.

The crucial part here is the conditional. If NUMA tiering is disabled, and the page isn't in the top tier, AND `last_cpupid` is *invalid*, then we halt the migration. The bug is that if the `last_cpupid` *is* valid, the migration can proceed even when tiering is off! The patch modifies this to `!(sysctl_numa_balancing_mode & NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING) && !node_is_toptier(src_nid)`, which seems to completely prevent migration from lower tier if tiering is off.

Now I'm getting into the heart of the matter. The question that's burning me is: when tiering is disabled, do we want to completely *disallow* all migrations from lower tiers, or just prevent *promotions*? The commit's behavior description makes it clear that the intended behavior is to restrict migrations "only between nodes within the same memory tier" when `NUMA_BALANCING_NORMAL` is enabled.

My concern is whether this change breaks normal NUMA balancing. If a task faults on a page in a lower-tier node (`src_nid`) while running on another lower-tier node (`dst_nid`), the normal balancing logic might want to migrate the page to the local node (`dst_nid`). However, the changed code unconditionally stops this migration from happening if the `src_nid` is a lower-tier node, thus invalidating this.

The crucial conditional `!node_is_toptier(src_nid)` is what I'm focused on. This check if the source node is lower-tier. The old code was more nuanced, but with this change, if the source node isn't a top-tier node, *no* migration is allowed. This is true even to another lower-tier node! This is a complete block. It effectively makes PMEM nodes act as isolated islands of memory.

Here's my immediate concern: the commit is intended to prevent *promotion* when tiering is disabled. However, by blocking all migration from a non-top-tier node, it prevents *any* NUMA migration, regardless of the destination. If PMEM is present, with this change, a PMEM page will NEVER migrate to a local PMEM node if a CPU that's accessing it is located on a PMEM node, and even worse, a PMEM page won't be able to migrate to a DRAM node.

Was this intended? I'm not convinced. The old code was intended to allow the migration if `last_cpupid` *was* valid, but this change prevents it unconditionally. Why was `!cpupid_valid(last_cpupid)` checked originally? I need to review the commit history; I will pull up the relevant `git log` now. I need to understand the intent of the original code, to see why we are seeing a regression, and to test this change thoroughly.

    "content": "{"content":"commit 33024536bafd9129f1d16ade0974671c648700ac\nAuthor: Ying Huang <huang.ying.caritas@gmail.com>\nDate:   Wed Jul 13 16:39:51 2022 +0800\n\n    memory tiering: hot page selection with hint page fault latency\n    \n    Patch series \"memory tiering: hot page selection\", v4.\n    \n    To optimize page placement in a memory tiering system with NUMA balancing,\n    the hot pages in the slow memory nodes need to be identified.\n    Essentially, the original NUMA balancing implementation selects the mostly\n    recently accessed (MRU) pages to promote.  But this isn't a perfect\n    algorithm to identify the hot pages.  Because the pages with quite low\n    access frequency may be accessed eventually given the NUMA balancing page\n    table scanning period could be quite long (e.g.  60 seconds).  So in this\n    patchset, we implement a new hot page identification algorithm based on\n    the latency between NUMA balancing page table scanning and hint page\n    fault.  Which is a kind of mostly frequently accessed (MFU) algorithm.\n    \n    In NUMA balancing memory tiering mode, if there are hot pages in slow\n    memory node and cold pages in fast memory node, we need to promote/demote\n    hot/cold pages between the fast and cold memory nodes.\n    \n    A choice is to promote/demote as fast as possible.  But the CPU cycles and\n    memory bandwidth consumed by the high promoting/demoting throughput will\n    hurt the latency of some workload because of accessing inflating and slow\n    memory bandwidth contention.\n    \n    A way to resolve this issue is to restrict the max promoting/demoting\n    throughput.  It will take longer to finish the promoting/demoting.  But\n    the workload latency will be better.  This is implemented in this patchset\n    as the page promotion rate limit mechanism.\n    \n    The promotion hot threshold is workload and system configuration\n    dependent.  So in this patchset, a method to adjust the hot threshold\n    automatically is implemented.  The basic idea is to control the number of\n    the candidate promotion pages to match the promotion rate limit.\n    \n    We used the pmbench memory accessing benchmark tested the patchset on a\n    2-socket server system with DRAM and PMEM installed.  The test results are\n    as follows,\n    \n                    pmbench score           promote rate\n                     (accesses/s)                   MB/s\n                    -------------           ------------\n    base              146887704.1                  725.6\n    hot selection     165695601.2                  544.0\n    rate limit        162814569.8                  165.2\n    auto adjustment   170495294.0                  136.9\n    \n    From the results above,\n    \n    With hot page selection patch [1/3], the pmbench score increases about\n    12.8%, and promote rate (overhead) decreases about 25.0%, compared with\n    base kernel.\n    \n    With rate limit patch [2/3], pmbench score decreases about 1.7%, and\n    promote rate decreases about 69.6%, compared with hot page selection\n    patch.\n    \n    With threshold auto adjustment patch [3/3], pmbench score increases about\n    4.7%, and promote rate decrease about 17.1%, compared with rate limit\n    patch.\n    \n    Baolin helped to test the patchset with MySQL on a machine which contains\n    1 DRAM node (30G) and 1 PMEM node (126G).\n    \n    sysbench /usr/share/sysbench/oltp_read_write.lua \\\n    ......\n    --tables=200 \\\n    --table-size=1000000 \\\n    --report-interval=10 \\\n    --threads=16 \\\n    --time=120\n    \n    The tps can be improved about 5%.\n    \n    \n    This patch (of 3):\n    \n    To optimize page placement in a memory tiering system with NUMA balancing,\n    the hot pages in the slow memory node need to be identified.  Essentially,\n    the original NUMA balancing implementation selects the mostly recently\n    accessed (MRU) pages to promote.  But this isn't a perfect algorithm to\n    identify the hot pages.  Because the pages with quite low access frequency\n    may be accessed eventually given the NUMA balancing page table scanning\n    period could be quite long (e.g.  60 seconds).  The most frequently\n    accessed (MFU) algorithm is better.\n    \n    So, in this patch we implemented a better hot page selection algorithm.\n    Which is based on NUMA balancing page table scanning and hint page fault\n    as follows,\n    \n    - When the page tables of the processes are scanned to change PTE/PMD\n      to be PROT_NONE, the current time is recorded in struct page as scan\n      time.\n    \n    - When the page is accessed, hint page fault will occur.  The scan\n      time is gotten from the struct page.  And The hint page fault\n      latency is defined as\n    \n        hint page fault time - scan time\n    \n    The shorter the hint page fault latency of a page is, the higher the\n    probability of their access frequency to be higher.  So the hint page\n    fault latency is a better estimation of the page hot/cold.\n    \n    It's hard to find some extra space in struct page to hold the scan time.\n    Fortunately, we can reuse some bits used by the original NUMA balancing.\n    \n    NUMA balancing uses some bits in struct page to store the page accessing\n    CPU and PID (referring to page_cpupid_xchg_last()).  Which is used by the\n    multi-stage node selection algorithm to avoid to migrate pages shared\n    accessed by the NUMA nodes back and forth.  But for pages in the slow\n    memory node, even if they are shared accessed by multiple NUMA nodes, as\n    long as the pages are hot, they need to be promoted to the fast memory\n    node.  So the accessing CPU and PID information are unnecessary for the\n    slow memory pages.  We can reuse these bits in struct page to record the\n    scan time.  For the fast memory pages, these bits are used as before.\n    \n    For the hot threshold, the default value is 1 second, which works well in\n    our performance test.  All pages with hint page fault latency < hot\n    threshold will be considered hot.\n    \n    It's hard for users to determine the hot threshold.  So we don't provide a\n    kernel ABI to set it, just provide a debugfs interface for advanced users\n    to experiment.  We will continue to work on a hot threshold automatic\n    adjustment mechanism.\n    \n    The downside of the above method is that the response time to the workload\n    hot spot changing may be much longer.  For example,\n    \n    - A previous cold memory area becomes hot\n    \n    - The hint page fault will be triggered.  But the hint page fault\n      latency isn't shorter than the hot threshold.  So the pages will\n      not be promoted.\n    \n    - When the memory area is scanned again, maybe after a scan period,\n      the hint page fault latency measured will be shorter than the hot\n      threshold and the pages will be promoted.\n    \n    To mitigate this, if there are enough free space in the fast memory node,\n    the hot threshold will not be used, all pages will be promoted upon the\n    hint page fault for fast response.\n    \n    Thanks Zhong Jiang reported and tested the fix for a bug when disabling\n    memory tiering mode dynamically.\n    \n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20220713083954.34196-1-ying.huang@intel.com\n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20220713083954.34196-2-ying.huang@intel.com\n    Signed-off-by: \"Huang, Ying\"     "thought": "**Analyzing NUMA Balancing with Memory Tiering**

Alright, let's break this down. I'm looking at commit 33024536bafd and I see some changes related to NUMA balancing, specifically how it interacts with memory tiering, and I think there's a problem in the implementation.  It looks like the `last_cpupid` field in `struct page` is repurposed. If `NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING` is enabled, it stores a scan timestamp on slow memory nodes, otherwise it stores a CPU ID, for fast pages. My mind immediately flashes red. What happens when we disable tiering dynamically? We could end up with pages in slow memory with timestamps still lurking in their `last_cpupid` field.

The original code included a check using `!cpupid_valid(last_cpupid)` to avoid misinterpreting a timestamp as a CPU ID when tiering is off. However, that check isn't foolproof, as the author admits. A timestamp *could* coincidentally look like a valid CPU ID. They want to *prevent* promotions when tiering is disabled. Their fix is an unconditional `return false` if the *source* node is a lower-tier node and tiering is disabled. This is where things get tricky.

This simple `return false` completely blocks migrations originating from lower tier nodes when `NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING` is disabled, even if `NUMA_BALANCING_NORMAL` is enabled! This means PMEM pages can *never* be migrated, even for normal NUMA balancing! The commit message is supposed to allow migration between nodes within the same memory tier. This seems contradictory.

Tracing `should_numa_migrate_memory` confirms the issue. The code only checks if the *source* node isn't a top-tier node, but it doesn't check if the *destination* node is a top-tier node. If you have two PMEM nodes and `NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING` is disabled, a fault on one PMEM node on a page on another PMEM node would be blocked, even if NUMA balancing is enabled! The commit message explicitly states "migration will occur only between nodes within the same memory tier," but the code *prevents* that.

What the author *intended* to prevent was promotion, not inter-tier migration in general. It looks like the goal is to disable promotion (from slow to fast memory), but the implementation accidentally disables ALL migrations from PMEM!  This doesn't seem correct.

I start to wonder if we're not treating the two different scenarios correctly. Should this `return false` occur only if the destination node is top tier? Perhaps the goal is to prevent any migration from slow to faster memory. Perhaps the original code was letting invalid timestamps cause mis-migration.

The core of the problem seems to be that if we allow lower-tier to lower-tier migration, there's a risk of using that garbage `last_cpupid` field as a real CPU ID for a single migration, but after the first fault, it's fixed. We need to be able to safely migrate from lower tier to lower tier and avoid promotion when memory tiering is disabled.

If the goal is to prevent *promotion*, the code should only block if the destination is top-tier.  We should do:

```c
	if (!(sysctl_numa_balancing_mode & NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING) &&
	    !node_is_toptier(src_nid) && node_is_toptier(dst_nid))
		return false;
```

This ensures we only prevent promotion, not general lower-tier to lower-tier migration, when NUMA memory tiering is disabled. Now that I understand the intention of the commit, and have some questions for the author, I believe this is what is desired, but not currently coded.

    "content": "{"content":"commit 33024536bafd9129f1d16ade0974671c648700ac\nAuthor: Ying Huang <huang.ying.caritas@gmail.com>\nDate:   Wed Jul 13 16:39:51 2022 +0800\n\n    memory tiering: hot page selection with hint page fault latency\n    \n    Patch series \"memory tiering: hot page selection\", v4.\n    \n    To optimize page placement in a memory tiering system with NUMA balancing,\n    the hot pages in the slow memory nodes need to be identified.\n    Essentially, the original NUMA balancing implementation selects the mostly\n    recently accessed (MRU) pages to promote.  But this isn't a perfect\n    algorithm to identify the hot pages.  Because the pages with quite low\n    access frequency may be accessed eventually given the NUMA balancing page\n    table scanning period could be quite long (e.g.  60 seconds).  So in this\n    patchset, we implement a new hot page identification algorithm based on\n    the latency between NUMA balancing page table scanning and hint page\n    fault.  Which is a kind of mostly frequently accessed (MFU) algorithm.\n    \n    In NUMA balancing memory tiering mode, if there are hot pages in slow\n    memory node and cold pages in fast memory node, we need to promote/demote\n    hot/cold pages between the fast and cold memory nodes.\n    \n    A choice is to promote/demote as fast as possible.  But the CPU cycles and\n    memory bandwidth consumed by the high promoting/demoting throughput will\n    hurt the latency of some workload because of accessing inflating and slow\n    memory bandwidth contention.\n    \n    A way to resolve this issue is to restrict the max promoting/demoting\n    throughput.  It will take longer to finish the promoting/demoting.  But\n    the workload latency will be better.  This is implemented in this patchset\n    as the page promotion rate limit mechanism.\n    \n    The promotion hot threshold is workload and system configuration\n    dependent.  So in this patchset, a method to adjust the hot threshold\n    automatically is implemented.  The basic idea is to control the number of\n    the candidate promotion pages to match the promotion rate limit.\n    \n    We used the pmbench memory accessing benchmark tested the patchset on a\n    2-socket server system with DRAM and PMEM installed.  The test results are\n    as follows,\n    \n                    pmbench score           promote rate\n                     (accesses/s)                   MB/s\n                    -------------           ------------\n    base              146887704.1                  725.6\n    hot selection     165695601.2                  544.0\n    rate limit        162814569.8                  165.2\n    auto adjustment   170495294.0                  136.9\n    \n    From the results above,\n    \n    With hot page selection patch [1/3], the pmbench score increases about\n    12.8%, and promote rate (overhead) decreases about 25.0%, compared with\n    base kernel.\n    \n    With rate limit patch [2/3], pmbench score decreases about 1.7%, and\n    promote rate decreases about 69.6%, compared with hot page selection\n    patch.\n    \n    With threshold auto adjustment patch [3/3], pmbench score increases about\n    4.7%, and promote rate decrease about 17.1%, compared with rate limit\n    patch.\n    \n    Baolin helped to test the patchset with MySQL on a machine which contains\n    1 DRAM node (30G) and 1 PMEM node (126G).\n    \n    sysbench /usr/share/sysbench/oltp_read_write.lua \\\n    ......\n    --tables=200 \\\n    --table-size=1000000 \\\n    --report-interval=10 \\\n    --threads=16 \\\n    --time=120\n    \n    The tps can be improved about 5%.\n    \n    \n    This patch (of 3):\n    \n    To optimize page placement in a memory tiering system with NUMA balancing,\n    the hot pages in the slow memory node need to be identified.  Essentially,\n    the original NUMA balancing implementation selects the mostly recently\n    accessed (MRU) pages to promote.  But this isn't a perfect algorithm to\n    identify the hot pages.  Because the pages with quite low access frequency\n    may be accessed eventually given the NUMA balancing page table scanning\n    period could be quite long (e.g.  60 seconds).  The most frequently\n    accessed (MFU) algorithm is better.\n    \n    So, in this patch we implemented a better hot page selection algorithm.\n    Which is based on NUMA balancing page table scanning and hint page fault\n    as follows,\n    \n    - When the page tables of the processes are scanned to change PTE/PMD\n      to be PROT_NONE, the current time is recorded in struct page as scan\n      time.\n    \n    - When the page is accessed, hint page fault will occur.  The scan\n      time is gotten from the struct page.  And The hint page fault\n      latency is defined as\n    \n        hint page fault time - scan time\n    \n    The shorter the hint page fault latency of a page is, the higher the\n    probability of their access frequency to be higher.  So the hint page\n    fault latency is a better estimation of the page hot/cold.\n    \n    It's hard to find some extra space in struct page to hold the scan time.\n    Fortunately, we can reuse some bits used by the original NUMA balancing.\n    \n    NUMA balancing uses some bits in struct page to store the page accessing\n    CPU and PID (referring to page_cpupid_xchg_last()).  Which is used by the\n    multi-stage node selection algorithm to avoid to migrate pages shared\n    accessed by the NUMA nodes back and forth.  But for pages in the slow\n    memory node, even if they are shared accessed by multiple NUMA nodes, as\n    long as the pages are hot, they need to be promoted to the fast memory\n    node.  So the accessing CPU and PID information are unnecessary for the\n    slow memory pages.  We can reuse these bits in struct page to record the\n    scan time.  For the fast memory pages, these bits are used as before.\n    \n    For the hot threshold, the default value is 1 second, which works well in\n    our performance test.  All pages with hint page fault latency < hot\n    threshold will be considered hot.\n    \n    It's hard for users to determine the hot threshold.  So we don't provide a\n    kernel ABI to set it, just provide a debugfs interface for advanced users\n    to experiment.  We will continue to work on a hot threshold automatic\n    adjustment mechanism.\n    \n    The downside of the above method is that the response time to the workload\n    hot spot changing may be much longer.  For example,\n    \n    - A previous cold memory area becomes hot\n    \n    - The hint page fault will be triggered.  But the hint page fault\n      latency isn't shorter than the hot threshold.  So the pages will\n      not be promoted.\n    \n    - When the memory area is scanned again, maybe after a scan period,\n      the hint page fault latency measured will be shorter than the hot\n      threshold and the pages will be promoted.\n    \n    To mitigate this, if there are enough free space in the fast memory node,\n    the hot threshold will not be used, all pages will be promoted upon the\n    hint page fault for fast response.\n    \n    Thanks Zhong Jiang reported and tested the fix for a bug when disabling\n    memory tiering mode dynamically.\n    \n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20220713083954.34196-1-ying.huang@intel.com\n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20220713083954.34196-2-ying.huang@intel.com\n    Signed-off-by: \"Huang, Ying\" <ying.huang@intel.com>\n    Reviewed-by: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>\n    Tested-by: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>\n    Cc: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>\n    Cc: Michal Hocko <mhocko@suse.com>\n    Cc: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>\n    Cc: Mel Gorman <mgorman@techsingularity.net>\n    Cc: Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>\n    Cc: Dave Hansen <dave.hansen@linux.intel.com>\n    Cc: Yang Shi <shy828301@gmail.com>\n    Cc: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>\n    Cc: Wei Xu <weixugc@google.com>\n    Cc: osalvador <osalvador@suse.de>\n    Cc: Shakeel Butt <shakeelb@google.com>\n    Cc: Zhong Jiang <zhongjiang-ali@linux.alibaba.com>\n    Cc: Oscar Salvador <osalvador@suse.de>\n    Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>\n"}",

Okay, so I'm digging into this NUMA balancing code, specifically the `should_numa_migrate_memory` function, and it's making my brain work. First, `node_is_toptier` is key; it's using the abstract distance to determine if a node is in the top tier. Memory tiers are defined by different abstract distances, and usually, CPU-less memory will have a higher distance.

The core of the issue: if `should_numa_migrate_memory` returns `false` when the source node (`src_nid`) isn't top-tier, and memory tiering is disabled, then NUMA balancing will *never* migrate from a lower-tier node, regardless of the target. But if NUMA balancing is enabled without tiering, should it migrate between two non-top-tier nodes? Say, two CXL nodes with distinct NUMA distances. Is this scenario even handled? Traditionally, NUMA balancing normal only migrates between top-tier nodes.

Let's check the commit message: "If NUMA_BALANCING_NORMAL is enabled, migration will occur only between nodes within the same memory tier." This implies *only* top-to-top nodes and low-to-low nodes. Does that mean that *if* lower-to-lower is allowed, the unconditional `return false` when `src_nid` is low-tier breaks that logic?

Let's look at the code:
```c
	if (!(sysctl_numa_balancing_mode & NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING) &&
	    !node_is_toptier(src_nid))
		return false;
```
If `src_nid` is lower-tier, `!node_is_toptier(src_nid)` evaluates to TRUE. When tiering is disabled, the first condition `!(...)` is also TRUE. So the function *always* returns `false`, preventing migration from a lower-tier node. Is this what the author meant by "between nodes within the same memory tier"?

Let's rewind. Before this patch, the code was:
```c
	if (!(sysctl_numa_balancing_mode & NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING) &&
	    !node_is_toptier(src_nid) && !cpupid_valid(last_cpupid))
		return false;
```
It would proceed if `last_cpupid` was valid. The code then checked:
```c
	if (!cpupid_pid_unset(last_cpupid) &&
				cpupid_to_nid(last_cpupid) != dst_nid)
		return false;

	/* Always allow migrate on private faults */
	if (cpupid_match_pid(p, last_cpupid))
		return true;
```
If it returned `true`, migration was allowed, but *to where*? To `dst_nid`, which is `cpu_to_node(dst_cpu)`. That's the node the task is running on.  CPU nodes *are* top-tier nodes. So, any migration triggered by `should_numa_migrate_memory` is a move *to* the task's node (`dst_nid`).

Now, if `src_nid` is lower-tier and `dst_nid` is top-tier (the CPU node), this is *always* a promotion. Is there even a case where `dst_nid` is lower-tier? In that case, the task would be running on a CPU in a lower-tier node, and that's extremely rare because lower-tier nodes either don't have CPUs or consider those CPUs to be top-tier.  `node_is_toptier` checks the abstract distance, so CPU nodes are almost always top-tier because memory directly attached to CPUs has a small distance.

Therefore, `should_numa_migrate_memory` *always* targets the CPU node. If `src_nid` is lower-tier, migrating to `dst_nid` (top-tier) *is* promotion. The commit message's claim that migration only occurs between nodes within the same memory tier is because if `src_nid` is top-tier, then it can migrate to the CPU node (also top-tier).  However, it can't move between a low-tier node to another low-tier node.

Ah! NUMA balancing normal takes a page fault when a task accesses a page, and then moves the page to the task's node. It only moves to the task's node, which is top-tier. It never moves lower-tier

    "content": "{"content":"commit 33024536bafd9129f1d16ade0974671c648700ac\nAuthor: Ying Huang <huang.ying.caritas@gmail.com>\nDate:   Wed Jul 13 16:39:51 2022 +0800\n\n    memory tiering: hot page selection with hint page fault latency\n    \n    Patch series \"memory tiering: hot page selection\", v4.\n    \n    To optimize page placement in a memory tiering system with NUMA balancing,\n    the hot pages in the slow memory nodes need to be identified.\n    Essentially, the original NUMA balancing implementation selects the mostly\n    recently accessed (MRU) pages to promote.  But this isn't a perfect\n    algorithm to identify the hot pages.  Because the pages with quite low\n    access frequency may be accessed eventually given the NUMA balancing page\n    table scanning period could be quite long (e.g.  60 seconds).  So in this\n    patchset, we implement a new hot page identification algorithm based on\n    the latency between NUMA balancing page table scanning and hint page\n    fault.  Which is a kind of mostly frequently accessed (MFU) algorithm.\n    \n    In NUMA balancing memory tiering mode, if there are hot pages in slow\n    memory node and cold pages in fast memory node, we need to promote/demote\n    hot/cold pages between the fast and cold memory nodes.\n    \n    A choice is to promote/demote as fast as possible.  But the CPU cycles and\n    memory bandwidth consumed by the high promoting/demoting throughput will\n    hurt the latency of some workload because of accessing inflating and slow\n    memory bandwidth contention.\n    \n    A way to resolve this issue is to restrict the max promoting/demoting\n    throughput.  It will take longer to finish the promoting/demoting.  But\n    the workload latency will be better.  This is implemented in this patchset\n    as the page promotion rate limit mechanism.\n    \n    The promotion hot threshold is workload and system configuration\n    dependent.  So in this patchset, a method to adjust the hot threshold\n    automatically is implemented.  The basic idea is to control the number of\n    the candidate promotion pages to match the promotion rate limit.\n    \n    We used the pmbench memory accessing benchmark tested the patchset on a\n    2-socket server system with DRAM and PMEM installed.  The test results are\n    as follows,\n    \n                    pmbench score           promote rate\n                     (accesses/s)                   MB/s\n                    -------------           ------------\n    base              146887704.1                  725.6\n    hot selection     165695601.2                  544.0\n    rate limit        162814569.8                  165.2\n    auto adjustment   170495294.0                  136.9\n    \n    From the results above,\n    \n    With hot page selection patch [1/3], the pmbench score increases about\n    12.8%, and promote rate (overhead) decreases about 25.0%, compared with\n    base kernel.\n    \n    With rate limit patch [2/3], pmbench score decreases about 1.7%, and\n    promote rate decreases about 69.6%, compared with hot page selection\n    patch.\n    \n    With threshold auto adjustment patch [3/3], pmbench score increases about\n    4.7%, and promote rate decrease about 17.1%, compared with rate limit\n    patch.\n    \n    Baolin helped to test the patchset with MySQL on a machine which contains\n    1 DRAM node (30G) and 1 PMEM node (126G).\n    \n    sysbench /usr/share/sysbench/oltp_read_write.lua \\\n    ......\n    --tables=200 \\\n    --table-size=1000000 \\\n    --report-interval=10 \\\n    --threads=16 \\\n    --time=120\n    \n    The tps can be improved about 5%.\n    \n    \n    This patch (of 3):\n    \n    To optimize page placement in a memory tiering system with NUMA balancing,\n    the hot pages in the slow memory node need to be identified.  Essentially,\n    the original NUMA balancing implementation selects the mostly recently\n    accessed (MRU) pages to promote.  But this isn't a perfect algorithm to\n    identify the hot pages.  Because the pages with quite low access frequency\n    may be accessed eventually given the NUMA balancing page table scanning\n    period could be quite long (e.g.  60 seconds).  The most frequently\n    accessed (MFU) algorithm is better.\n    \n    So, in this patch we implemented a better hot page selection algorithm.\n    Which is based on NUMA balancing page table scanning and hint page fault\n    as follows,\n    \n    - When the page tables of the processes are scanned to change PTE/PMD\n      to be PROT_NONE, the current time is recorded in struct page as scan\n      time.\n    \n    - When the page is accessed, hint page fault will occur.  The scan\n      time is gotten from the struct page.  And The hint page fault\n      latency is defined as\n    \n        hint page fault time - scan time\n    \n    The shorter the hint page fault latency of a page is, the higher the\n    probability of their access frequency to be higher.  So the hint page\n    fault latency is a better estimation of the page hot/cold.\n    \n    It's hard to find some extra space in struct page to hold the scan time.\n    Fortunately, we can reuse some bits used by the original NUMA balancing.\n    \n    NUMA balancing uses some bits in struct page to store the page accessing\n    CPU and PID (referring to page_cpupid_xchg_last()).  Which is used by the\n    multi-stage node selection algorithm to avoid to migrate pages shared\n    accessed by the NUMA nodes back and forth.  But for pages in the slow\n    memory node, even if they are shared accessed by multiple NUMA nodes, as\n    long as the pages are hot, they need to be promoted to the fast memory\n    node.  So the accessing CPU and PID information are unnecessary for the\n    slow memory pages.  We can reuse these bits in struct page to record the\n    scan time.  For the fast memory pages, these bits are used as before.\n    \n    For the hot threshold, the default value is 1 second, which works well in\n    our performance test.  All pages with hint page fault latency < hot\n    threshold will be considered hot.\n    \n    It's hard for users to determine the hot threshold.  So we don't provide a\n    kernel ABI to set it, just provide a debugfs interface for advanced users\n    to experiment.  We will continue to work on a hot threshold automatic\n    adjustment mechanism.\n    \n    The downside of the above method is that the response time to the workload\n    hot spot changing may be much longer.  For example,\n    \n    - A previous cold memory area becomes hot\n    \n    - The hint page fault will be triggered.  But the hint page fault\n      latency isn't shorter than the hot threshold.  So the pages will\n      not be promoted.\n    \n    - When the memory area is scanned again, maybe after a scan period,\n      the hint page fault latency measured will be shorter than the hot\n      threshold and the pages will be promoted.\n    \n    To mitigate this, if there are enough free space in the fast memory node,\n    the hot threshold will not be used, all pages will be promoted upon the\n    hint page fault for fast response.\n    \n    Thanks Zhong Jiang reported and tested the fix for a bug when disabling\n    memory tiering mode dynamically.\n    \n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20220713083954.34196-1-ying.huang@intel.com\n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20220713083954.34196-2-ying.huang@intel.com\n    Signed-off-by: \"Huang, Ying\" <ying.huang@intel.com>\n    Reviewed-by: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>\n    Tested-by: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>\n    Cc: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>\n    Cc: Michal Hocko <mhocko@suse.com>\n    Cc: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>\n    Cc: Mel Gorman <mgorman@techsingularity.net>\n    Cc: Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>\n    Cc: Dave Hansen <dave.hansen@linux.intel.com>\n    Cc: Yang Shi <shy828301@gmail.com>\n    Cc: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>\n    Cc: Wei Xu <weixugc@google.com>\n    Cc: osalvador <osalvador@suse.de>\n    Cc: Shakeel Butt <shakeelb@google.com>\n    Cc: Zhong Jiang <zhongjiang-ali@linux.alibaba.com>\n    Cc: Oscar Salvador <osalvador@suse.de>\n    Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>\n"}",
    "role": "tool",
    "tool_call_id": "git_show"

    "thought": "**Analyzing NUMA Balancing and Memory Tiering Logic**

Okay, so I'm looking at this code and trying to piece together the implications of this commit. It seems like a recent change, specifically adding the `!cpupid_valid(last_cpupid)` check, was introduced because `last_cpupid` was repurposed. Originally, it stored the last CPU/PID accessing a page, but in the context of memory tiering, it's now being used to track the scan time for lower-tier pages.

Right, the commit notes: "NUMA balancing uses some bits in struct page to store the page accessing CPU and PID... But for pages in the slow memory node... the accessing CPU and PID information are unnecessary... We can reuse these bits in struct page to record the scan time." So, that makes sense. For lower-tier nodes, `last_cpupid` *is* actually the scan time instead of CPU/PID.

The critical issue arises when `NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING` is dynamically disabled. If this happens, the bits previously used for the scan time could be interpreted as a *valid* `cpupid`! The system then reverts to normal NUMA balancing, which expects `last_cpupid` to contain CPU/PID information, but it's likely still holding scan time values!

The purpose of `!cpupid_valid(last_cpupid)` was, in theory, to filter out these invalid CPU/PIDs, and catch a potential migration of a low-tier page. But wait, scan time values could *accidentally* register as valid CPU/PIDs! So the existing check was flawed!

Ah, Ying Huang's patch:
```c
	if (!(sysctl_numa_balancing_mode & NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING) &&
	    !node_is_toptier(src_nid) && !cpupid_valid(last_cpupid))
```

    "thought": "**Analyzing NUMA Tiering and Memory Migration Logic**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial concern was about the interaction between disabling tiering and how `task_numa_fault` and `should_numa_migrate_memory` behave. If tiering is off, `task_numa_fault` still *records* faults even on lower tiers, as long as `last_cpupid` is valid. That seemed odd, since `should_numa_migrate_memory` was designed to prevent actual migration of lower tier pages when tiering is disabled. I needed to understand if these operations were consistent.

I had a moment of confusion because the code unconditionally returns false in `should_numa_migrate_memory` for lower-tier pages when tiering is disabled. That appears to prevent promotion.

However, after further scrutiny of the logic, the behavior seems correct. When NUMA balancing is in its normal mode, migration *should* occur within the same tier. If a page is on a lower-tier node, and tiering is disabled, it will never migrate to a top-tier node. This is because the destination CPU is almost always on a top-tier node, because CPU nodes are by definition on a top tier node, and `dst_nid` is top tier. This guarantees that destination nodes are top tier nodes. The check in `should_numa_migrate_memory` mirrors the intent to prevent promotion to a higher tier. If we're on a lower tier, staying there is fine in this mode.

Now, a critical point emerged: the code *does* update `last_cpupid` even when `should_numa_migrate_memory` returns `false` (because tiering is off and the page is on a lower tier). The reasoning here is also valid. This doesn't seem unsafe. We are merely recording access information. The important question is what happens if tiering is later enabled.

Here's the problem: The variable `last_cpupid` stores a CPU/PID pair when tiering is disabled. That data is then read as an access time, resulting in a garbage calculation for the page fault's latency.

However, this is not a new issue. The existing code, *before* the patch, also stored the CPU/PID pair in the folio, for one read. It's essentially a one-time glitch, a transient issue during the very first fault after tiering gets re-enabled. After this first read, the system will start working correctly. This single, likely acceptable, problem of one fault per page, is unlikely to cause a great issue.

Finally, I checked `migrate_misplaced_folio` to confirm that returning `false` from `should_numa_migrate_memory` doesn't cause leaks. No leaks or issues are present.

Okay, here's what's going through my head as I dissect this code. First, I see this conditional assignment:

```c
	if (folio_use_access_time(folio))
		*last_cpupid = (-1 & LAST_CPUPID_MASK);
	else
		*last_cpupid = folio_last_cpupid(folio);
```

Right, so `last_cpupid` is being extracted from the folio *before* the `mpol_misplaced` function is called, which in turn calls `should_numa_migrate_memory`. Hmm, that seems... important. Wait, then the value of `last_cpupid` being passed to `task_numa_fault` is the value *before* `should_numa_migrate_memory` might update it with `this_cpupid`. Interesting.

Now, if `NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING` is disabled, `folio_use_access_time(folio)` evaluates to `FALSE`. Therefore, `*last_cpupid = folio_last_cpupid(folio);` is executed. This reads bits from the folio *before* they're potentially updated to `this_cpupid`. If that folio was previously using those bits for access time because tiering *was* enabled, those bits might form what looks like a valid `cpupid`!

And then, `task_numa_fault(last_cpupid, nid, nr_pages, flags);` gets called. Let's see... in `task_numa_fault`:

```c
	if (!node_is_toptier(mem_node) &&
	    (sysctl_numa_balancing_mode & NUMA_BALANCING_MEMORY_TIERING ||
	     !cpupid_valid(last_cpupid)))
		return;
```

Ah, right, if tiering is *disabled*, and that `last_cpupid` happens to be considered VALID, `task_numa_fault` will *not* return! It will proceed! And then the fault will be attributed to this `last_cpupid`. But... `last_cpupid` is actually an ACCESS TIME that accidentally looks like a valid `cpupid`. allowing normal NUMA balancing migrations within the same tier.
